answers.value: The STEM Makerspace Storytelling Lab will integrate hands-on engineering, coding, and digital media production into 6th–8th grade English Language Arts. Students will design and build simple interactive models (using microcontrollers and recycled materials) that retell key scenes from novels and historical texts. They will script, film, and edit short explanatory videos, then showcase their projects at a family STEM night. The grant funds will support microcontroller kits, low-cost robotics components, green screen equipment, and basic video production tools, as well as consumable building materials for three grade levels.

answers.value:
The primary target group consists of approximately 126 students in grades 6 through 8 at Riverside Middle School who are enrolled in standard and co-taught English Language Arts classes. This includes:

- Three sections of 6th grade ELA (about 63 students total), with a mix of students reading on, above, and below grade level.
- Two sections of 7th grade ELA (about 42 students total), including several English learners at WIDA levels 2–4 and students receiving reading intervention services.
- One section of 8th grade ELA (about 21 students), designated as a project-based learning cohort that includes students with IEPs and 504 plans for ADHD, dyslexia, and mild anxiety.

The group is intentionally diverse in terms of academic performance, language background (primarily English and Spanish, with a small number of Arabic speakers), and learning needs. About 68% of students qualify for free or reduced-price lunch, and several have limited access to technology or creative materials at home. The lab is designed to provide these students with equitable access to hands-on STEM experiences, opportunities to build confidence as creators rather than just consumers of media, and structured collaboration practice through clearly defined roles (project manager, lead builder, coder, and scriptwriter) within each small group.
